actions.thought: In the first hand of the tournament, I am in the Button/SB position with 8d 4c. This is a very weak starting hand, even for heads-up play. While opening a wide range is standard in this position, 84o sits at the bottom of the range and often doesn't realize its equity well. I will fold this hand to preserve my stack and observe my opponent's tendencies before committing chips with marginal holdings.

Current stack depth is 100BB. In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) typically opens a wide range (approx. 70-80% of hands). However, 76o (Seven-Six offsuit) lies near the bottom of this opening range. It lacks the playability of suited connectors and the showdown value of high-card hands. While some exploitative strategies might advocate raising 'any two cards' until the opponent adjusts, a solid GTO baseline at this stack depth generally folds 76o to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots, especially this early in the tournament (Hand 2) without reads on the opponent's defense frequencies. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the Small Blind and it only costs 50 more to call, the hand has almost no post-flop potential. Given it is early in the tournament, there is no reason to waste chips on a hand with such low equity.

In a Heads-Up match 100bb deep, K3o is a marginal hand but generally strong enough to open from the Button given the positional advantage. With the opponent folding in 2 of the first 3 hands, an aggressive strategy is favored to steal the blind. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to define the range, take down the pot pre-flop if the opponent is tight, or play in position with the betting lead.

I am in the Big Blind holding K7o. The opponent in the Small Blind has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 400 total. Getting 3:1 pot odds, K7o is a standard defend against a wide SB opening range. I will call and play the hand post-flop.

The flop is extremely dry with a pair of Queens and a deuce. Having missed the board with my K7o, I will follow standard procedure and check to the pre-flop aggressor. King-high may still be the best hand or have some showdown value, but there is no reason to lead into the raiser on this texture.

The board texture Q-Q-2 is very dry and static, favoring my range as the pre-flop aggressor since I hold all the strong Queen combinations (AQ, KQ), while the opponent's defending range contains many missed hands. Although King-high has some showdown value, a small continuation bet (c-bet) is effective here for two reasons: 1) It capitalizes on the opponent's passive history (folding frequently pre-flop) to take the pot down immediately against their 'fit-or-fold' tendencies, and 2) It denies equity to random lower cards that still have live outs against my weak K3. A small bet size of roughly 1/3 pot is sufficient to accomplish this on a paired board.

On this extremely dry paired flop of Qc-Qs-2h, the opponent is likely continuation-betting a wide range. My King-high (K7o) has significant showdown value and is currently ahead of many of the opponent's bluffs or lower high-card hands. The bet size of 150 into 400 offers me excellent pot odds (3.67:1). Folding here would be overly exploitable, and raising turns a hand with showdown value into a bluff. I will call to see how the turn develops.

The turn 6d is a total brick and does not change the board texture significantly. Having checked and called a small bet on the flop with King-high, my hand still retains some showdown value against the opponent's air and wide range of bluffs. However, leading out here (donking) makes little sense as it would likely only be called by better hands and would force out the bluffs I want to keep in. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. If they check back, I'm happy to go to the river; if they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing.

The board is now [Qc Qs 2h 6d]. The 6d is a brick that does not significantly alter the board texture or complete flush draws. Having c-bet the flop and received a call, the opponent's range likely consists of a Queen (trips), pocket pairs (22-JJ), a Deuce, or floats with Ace-high/broadway cards. My hand (Kh 3s) has marginal showdown value. Betting again (double barreling) is risky because better hands (pairs, trips) will likely call, and worse hands (missed floats) might fold, meaning I gain little value and risk being raised or calling a large bet on the river with weak holdings. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (seeing the river for free), and potentially win at showdown against missed draws or lower high cards. If the opponent has a trap, checking avoids losing more chips.

The river is the 8h, which is a fairly blank card. The turn went check-check, which suggests that the opponent likely does not have a Queen or a very strong pocket pair. My King-high has a decent amount of showdown value against the opponent's missed high cards or total air. There is no reason to bet here, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse or fold out a better hand. I will check and see if my King-high is good enough to win the pot at showdown.

My hand (Kh 3s) plays as a Pair of Queens with a King kicker on the [Qc Qs 2h 6d 8h] board. I lose to any Ace-high, any pocket pair, or any board pair (2x, 6x, 8x), and I only beat missed broadway floats (like J-high). The opponent called a flop c-bet on a paired board and checked down, suggesting they likely hold a marginal made hand (bottom pair, pocket pair) or Ace-high, which they are unlikely to fold to a river bet after I checked the turn (showing weakness). Turning my hand into a bluff here is risky against a passive opponent who may call with any pair. Checking back realizes my equity against worse floats and loses the minimum against better hands.

Q9o is a solid starting hand in a heads-up match from the button. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take the initiative in the hand, aiming to win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play with position.

I hold A8o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. In heads-up play, A8o is well above the median hand strength and definitely strong enough to defend. The price to call is 200 into a pot of 400, offering me 3:1 odds. 3-betting is an option, but it bloats the pot out of position with a hand that has mediocre playability. Folding is too weak given the hand strength relative to a wide button opening range. Calling allows me to realize equity and re-evaluate on the flop.

I am out of position on the flop with Ace-high (Ac8d) on a board of Jd 2d 3c. The standard play for the pre-flop caller is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose as I would likely only fold out worse hands and isolate myself against better ones (pairs or strong draws). I have some equity with an overcard and backdoor diamond potential, so I want to keep the pot small and see what the opponent chooses to do. Checking allows me to evaluate their action; if they check back, I realize equity for free. If they bet, I will decide based on the sizing.

I have position and the pre-flop lead. The flop of 2d-Jd-3c is relatively dry but contains a flush draw. Since I raised pre-flop, I should continue with a standard continuation bet to represent a Jack or a strong overpair. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a balanced sizing that applies pressure to the opponent's non-made hands and draws while allowing me to fold without too much loss if they show significant strength.

I hold Ace-high (Ac8d) on a Jd 2d 3c board. My opponent, playing the Button in a heads-up match, raised pre-flop and has now continuation-bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). In heads-up poker, ranges are very wide, and an Ace-high hand often has showdown value against the opponent's bluffs (air, lower overcards, missed straight draws). I also hold the 8d, giving me a backdoor flush draw, plus an overcard (Ace). I need 25% equity to call (300 to win 1200). Against a wide c-betting range that includes many semi-bluffs and air hands, my hand easily meets this equity threshold. Raising would isolate me against better hands, and folding is too weak given the heads-up dynamic and my specific holding. Calling allows me to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am out of position on the turn with Ace-high (Ac8d) on a board of Jd 2d 3c Tc. The Ten of Clubs is a card that connects well with the opponent's button opening range (hitting hands like JT, AT, KT, QT, T9). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only fold out hands I am currently beating (pure bluffs) while getting action from any pair or better, effectively isolating myself against a stronger range. My Ace-high has some showdown value if the opponent checks back, and checking gives me the option to evaluate his action—whether he decides to barrel again or take a free card. Therefore, checking is the standard and optimal play to minimize loss and potentially realize equity at showdown.

Pro has checked twice, indicating potential weakness or a trapping hand like a Jack or a draw. The turn Ten of Clubs gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Since I have some showdown value with Queen-high and a drawing opportunity, checking back is a solid play to control the pot size and see the river for free. This avoids a potential check-raise and allows me to evaluate the river action based on the final card.

I hold Ace-high (Ac8d) on a river board of Jd 2d 3c Tc 4c. The river brings a third club, making a flush possible. I hold the Ace of Clubs, which is the nut flush blocker, but my actual hand value is only Ace-high. The opponent raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn, which indicates weakness or pot control with a marginal made hand (like Jx or Tx) or a draw. If I bet, I likely fold out all the missed draws and air that I am currently beating, while only getting called by pairs that beat me. Turning my hand into a bluff is risky as heads-up opponents may call down light with top or middle pair. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against his missed draws or air, maximizing the value of my hand.

On the river, the board is 2d-Jd-3c-Tc-4c. I have Queen-high, which has very little showdown value against a range that called a flop continuation bet. However, Pro has checked for the third time (including the turn check). My check on the turn likely signaled that I was either pot-controlling a marginal hand or giving up on a bluff. By checking back now, I can see the showdown for free. While I might be able to bluff Pro off a weak pair with a large bet, the risk of being called by a Jack or a Ten is high given the board texture. Since it's early in the match, I'll take the free showdown and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 73o, which is one of the worst starting hands in heads-up poker. Although playing heads-up requires a wide range, 73o falls into the bottom percentile that is typically folded. I have folded the SB once (Hand 2 with 76o) and raised once (Hand 4 with K3o). While I should be careful not to fold too often to prevent the opponent from auto-profiting, 73o is not the hand to balance my frequencies with, as it has very poor playability and equity out of position. Saving chips for a playable spot is the prudent play here.

In heads-up play, Ten-Jack offsuit (Td Js) is a strong starting hand from the Button/Small Blind position. I want to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 9d facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). K9o is a solid defending hand heads-up, having sufficient equity against a typical opening range. I need to call 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. Folding would be too tight, and while a 3-bet is possible, calling is the standard play to realize equity and keep the opponent's range wide. I will call to see a flop.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a 9 kicker on a Kd-8d-4h board. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows the opponent to c-bet with their air, draws, or worse value hands. Leading out would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against a stronger range. I plan to check-call any reasonable bet.

As the pre-flop aggressor on a King-high board, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative. This board texture favors my perceived range more than the caller's. A half-pot bet puts pressure on the opponent's weaker holdings, such as middle pairs or draws, and allows me to win the pot immediately if they missed completely.

I hold Top Pair with a 9 kicker (Kc9d) on a Kd-8d-4h board. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) from the Button, I have a clear continue. Folding is impossible given the strength of my hand heads-up. Raising (check-raising) has merit to charge flush draws and get value from worse Kings, but it risks isolating me against better hands (AK, KQ, sets) and folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Calling is the most balanced line to control the pot size out of position and allow the opponent to fire again on the turn with draws or air.

On the Turn, the board is Kd 8d 4h 4s. The 4s pairs the board, which is relatively blank for the ranges involved, though it makes trips for any random 4x (unlikely raise-call holding). I still have Top Pair with a decent kicker. I am out of position. The pot is 1200. Checking is the standard line here to allow the opponent to continue bluffing with flush draws or lower pairs, or to value bet worse Kings. Leading out would fold out his bluffs. I will check and evaluate his action.

The turn 4s is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. After my flop continuation bet was called, it's likely the opponent has a piece of the board, such as a King, an Eight, or a diamond draw. Since I currently hold only Jack-high and have no immediate draw, betting again (double-barreling) is risky against a range that seems to have connected. I will check behind to control the pot size and see a free river card, which could potentially improve my hand or allow me to evaluate the opponent's river action.

On a board of Kd-8d-4h-4s-4c, I hold a Full House (Fours full of Kings) with Kc-9d. The opponent's line—raising pre-flop, c-betting the flop, then checking back the turn—suggests a capped range consisting of marginal made hands (like QQ-99 or a weak King) or missed flush draws. He is unlikely to hold a strong King or AA, as those would typically value bet the turn. On the river, I beat all pocket pairs below Kings and chop with any King. Checking would likely lead to the opponent checking back his underpairs (QQ-99) for showdown value. By betting small (1/3 pot), I can extract value from these underpairs which might call thinking I am bluffing with a missed draw. While checking might induce a bluff from his air, the 'trip 4s' board texture usually discourages bluffing. Therefore, a value bet is the best play to target his marginal holdings.

The board has completed with triple fours, meaning the best hand is formed by those three fours and the two highest cards available. My current best hand is 4-4-4-K-J. While this beats the board's default 4-4-4-K-8, I am losing to any King (full house), any pocket pair (full house), and any Ace-high or Queen-high. Pro called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests they have a piece of this board (like a King or an Eight) or a pocket pair. Pro's lead on the river after I checked the turn is a sign of strength or a thin value bet. Given my Jack-high is a very weak bluff-catcher in this spot, I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Kh3s, the exact same hand I held in Hand #4. In Heads-Up play, King-high hands have decent equity and are typically strong enough to open, either by limping or raising, to steal the blind or play in position. In Hand #4, I raised to 200 with this hand. To maintain consistency and pressure on the Big Blind, and given that K3o is generally within a standard opening range (top ~45%) for heads-up, I will raise to 200 again. This allows me to take down the pot pre-flop if the opponent folds or play a pot in position with initiative.

I am holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand in any poker format and especially strong in heads-up play. Pro has opened for 200 from the Button. I am in the Big Blind and should look to 3-bet (re-raise) for value and to take control of the pot, particularly since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Raising to 700 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on Pro's opening range and builds a pot while I likely hold a significant equity advantage.

I hold Kh3s on the Button/SB. After opening to 200, the opponent (Flash) has 3-bet to 700. Although I would be in position post-flop, K3o is a weak hand that is often dominated by a 3-betting range (which likely consists of strong Ax, pairs, or better Kx). The price to call is 500 into a pot of 900, requiring about 26% raw equity, but realized equity will be lower due to the weak kicker and lack of connectivity. Without specific reads that the opponent is 3-betting excessively wide this early in the match (Hand 8), the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

3s 5c is near the bottom of starting hands and lacks significant playability or high-card value. While heads-up strategy often involves opening a wide range from the Small Blind, this hand is weak enough to fold, especially against a competent opponent where I have no clear advantage post-flop with such a hand.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit (KQo) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Big Blind's random hand range. In previous hands (specifically Hand #4 and #8), I have established a precedent of min-raising to 200. To disguise the strength of my hand and maintain consistency with my range, a standard min-raise is the most effective play here. This action builds the pot while in position and puts pressure on the opponent, potentially inducing a call from dominated hands like Qx or Kx with weaker kickers.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the remaining 100 chips, the hand has very poor equity realization and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. It is more profitable to fold now and wait for a better spot than to defend with such a weak range of cards.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. In heads-up play, an Ace-high hand is well above the average starting hand. I have opened for 300 in previous hands which has resulted in folds or post-flop play where I can maintain initiative. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7d 5h. My opponent (Flash) in the Small Blind/Button has raised to 300 (3bb). This is a larger sizing than a min-raise, giving me worse pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity). 75o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and falls below the standard defense range against a 3x open. Given the price and hand strength, the best move is to fold.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) on the Button/SB, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy is to build the pot for value while protecting against random holdings the Big Blind might have. A min-raise to 200 is my standard opening size, consistent with previous hands (Hand #4, #8, #10), allowing me to balance this premium hand with my wider stealing range. If the opponent 3-bets, I am well-positioned to call or 4-bet.

I am holding 94o in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 94o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Against a competent opponent, calling frequently leads to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated or forced to fold. Given it is early in the session and I want to avoid high-variance situations with marginal holdings, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

K5o is a decent hand to open from the button in heads-up play. Based on my previous play style in this session, I have consistently opened for 300 (3x the big blind) from the button. This allows me to take initiative and leverage my positional advantage post-flop if the Big Blind calls.

I hold QJo in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. This is a standard defense situation in heads-up play. QJo is well ahead of a random hand and performs well against a wide button opening range. I am getting 2:1 on a call (calling 200 to win 400). Folding is too tight, and while a 3-bet is possible, calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize equity in position post-flop (if check-calling) or navigate the board relative to my hand strength. The opponent has been opening 3x frequently, suggesting a standard aggressive strategy. I will call.

I am out of position on an A-7-5 board with Queen-high and a backdoor flush draw. The Ace-high texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Leading (donk betting) here would be strategically unsound as it allows the opponent to play perfectly against me. The standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-high board after raising pre-flop. While the Ace is in the opponent's calling range, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue to represent strength. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) can win the pot immediately if the opponent missed the flop or has a weak draw. If called, I have some showdown value and can look to check back on the turn to control the pot size.

I hold QJo on a 5s-Ac-7c flop. I checked, and the opponent bet 300 into a pot of 600. The Ace-high board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as they retain all strong Ax hands. My hand has no pair, no immediate straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush draw. I am drawing very thin against any Ace, and my overcards (Q, J) are not guaranteed to be clean outs even if I hit them. The pot odds of 3:1 (25% equity required) are not sufficient for a hand with such low playability and equity out of position. Floating here would be a leak. The disciplined play is to fold and move to the next hand.

I hold Ace-Jack suited (Ah Jh) on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, this is a premium hand. My standard opening size has been a min-raise to 200, which I used in previous hands with varying strength. Raising to 200 allows me to build the pot for value while balancing my range, as I would make this same move with weaker holdings. I want to encourage a call from worse hands or take down the blinds immediately.

I am in the big blind with 8c4c. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 200 total, which means I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This provides me with 3-to-1 pot odds. While 84 suited is a weak hand, it has some post-flop playability and I have the odds to defend against a standard open. Folding here would be too tight given the price, so I will call and see a flop.

I have a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). On this texture, I have plenty of equity against most of the opponent's range. Since I am first to act post-flop as the Big Blind, I will start with a check to see how the opponent reacts to this coordinated board. If they bet, I have the option to check-call or check-raise depending on the sizing.

I hold AhJh on a board of 7c 5c 9d. Although I have two overcards and range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, this texture (low, connected, flush draw) connects very well with the Big Blind's calling range. Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would force me to fold significant equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and keeps my checking range protected with strong Ace-highs. I have sufficient showdown value to see a free turn.

I have completed my flush on the turn with the Queen of Clubs. On the flop, both players checked, which often indicates the opponent doesn't have a dominant hand but might have something worth calling a value bet. I will lead out for half the pot to start building value and potentially get called by a Queen, a nine, or a high club draw.

The board is 7c 5c 9d Qc. The turn card brings a third club, completing potential flushes, and is also an overcard that connects well with the Big Blind's defense range (Qx, two pair). My hand (Ah Jh) has no pair and no club, leaving me drawing nearly dead against flushes and way behind any pair. While the bet is small (half-pot), my equity is low, and I cannot confidently call a river bet or win at showdown without improving. The prudent play is to preserve chips.
In heads-up play, K3o is a reasonable hand to open from the Button/Small Blind to apply pressure and take control of the pot in position. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3x BB) from the Button/Small Blind. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds (requiring 33% equity) and J6s has roughly 39-43% raw equity against a wide heads-up opening range, the larger raise size combined with my position (Out of Position) significantly reduces my ability to realize this equity. The opponent has shown a tendency to c-bet frequently (100% in observed hands), making it difficult to continue without connecting well. Against a 3x open, J6s falls into the bottom of the defending range or a fold. Given the aggressive post-flop tendencies and the price, I will make a disciplined fold to preserve my stack.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit (Ac Td) on the Button/Small Blind. In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand, well within the top range of hands. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression (3-betting in Hand #8) but also folds to aggression. A min-raise to 200 is the standard play here to build the pot for value, deny equity to the Big Blind's random holdings, and utilize my position post-flop. I expect to have the best hand frequently.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The opponent raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the early stage of the match, I prefer to fold these types of bottom-tier hands and wait for better spots rather than potentially bleeding chips post-flop.

I am on the button with Ace-Tray offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button in previous hands, and I will maintain that sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot with a hand that has significant high-card value.

I hold Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. This appears to be their standard opening size. QTo is a strong enough hand to defend heads-up, ranking well within the top 30-40% of hands. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into a pot that will be 600, offering 3:1). Folding would be too tight given the width of button opening ranges in heads-up play. While a 3-bet is an option, calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize equity without inflating the pot out of position with a medium-strength hand. I will call the additional 200 chips.

I hold middle pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a board of Kd Td 5d. I am out of position in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor. The board texture contains a King and a flush draw, which connects reasonably well with the opponent's button opening range. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Kings) or strong draws, while folding out weaker hands that I dominate. Checking is the standard play here to keep the opponent's range wide, induce bluffs, and control the size of the pot with a medium-strength hand. I intend to check-call if the opponent bets a standard size.

I have the nut flush draw with the Ace of Diamonds and one overcard. On a King-high board where I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard. Betting 300 (half-pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately if the opponent missed, while building a larger pot for the times I improve to the nut flush or an Ace on later streets. Holding the Ad also blocks many of the opponent's continuing ranges involving diamonds.

I hold middle pair (Tens) with a good kicker (Queen) on a King-high board with a flush draw present (Td Kh 5d). The opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600). Given the wide opening range of the button in heads-up play, the opponent likely c-bets this texture with a mix of top pairs (Kings), draws (flush draws, straight draws like QJ), and pure bluffs/air. My hand is currently ahead of their bluffs and draws, and only behind King-x or better made hands. Raising would likely isolate me against a stronger range, whereas folding is too weak given the pot odds (25%) and my relative hand strength. Calling keeps their bluffing range in play and controls the pot size while out of position.

I hold middle pair (Tens) and a gutshot straight draw (Jack gives K-Q-J-T-9) on a board of Kd Td 5d 9s. The turn card (9s) completes a straight for QJ and brings potential two-pair combinations (T9, K9), making the board wetter. Being out of position with a medium-strength hand, leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as it isolates me against better hands (Kx, sets, two pair) and folds out the bluffs I am currently beating. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, potentially see a free river, or evaluate the opponent's sizing if they choose to double-barrel.

I have the nut flush draw with the Ace of Diamonds and one overcard. Pro called my flop continuation bet, indicating they likely have a piece of this board, such as a King, a Ten, or a straight draw. The Nine of Spades on the turn is a relatively neutral card, but it does complete some straight draws like QJ. Since Pro has checked again, I have the option to take a free card to try and realize my equity. Betting again risks a check-raise which would force me to fold my high equity draw. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, where I have 12 outs (9 diamonds and 3 Aces) to potentially take the lead.

I hold middle pair (Tens) on a board of Kd Td 5d 9s 6s. The opponent raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn. The turn check-back often indicates a capped range—either a weak made hand (weak King, Ten, or mid-pair) trying to get to showdown cheaply, or a draw taking a free card. The river bricked the diamond flush draw. Betting here is thin for value because better hands (Kings) will call, and worse hands (missed draws, small pairs) will likely fold. Checking allows me to induce bluffs from missed draws or get to showdown cheaply against marginal hands. I have sufficient showdown value to win against their missed draws and lower pairs, so I will check and evaluate if facing a bet.

I have Ace-high with the Ace of Diamonds. The board is King-high and I missed both my flush draw and my overcard. Pro has checked the river after also checking the turn. While I could represent a King or a straight (QJ), Pro's line of check-calling the flop and then checking the rest of the way often indicates a marginal made hand like a Ten or a small pair that may not fold to a single river bet. In heads-up play, Ace-high occasionally wins at showdown against missed lower draws.
